3 min read
2 hours ago

--

Learning React is exciting.
You build your first component, see something appear on the screen, and suddenly it feels like you’re creating real applications.
But after that honeymoon phase, reality kicks in.
Your components become harder to manage. Your app starts behaving unexpectedly. You spend hours debugging errors that turn out to be caused by a tiny mistake.
The good news?
Almost every React developer has made these mistakes — including me.
If you’re just getting started with React, avoiding these five common mistakes will save you countless hours of frustration.

1. Mutating State Directly
This is one of the most common mistakes beginners make.
Imagine you have an array stored in state.
Instead of creating a new array, you modify the existing one directly.
React relies on state changes to know when it should re-render a component. If you mutate the existing state, React may not detect the update correctly, leading to bugs that are difficult to trace.
Better approach
Always create a new object or array when updating state.
Think of React state as read-only. Instead of changing it, replace it with a new version.

2. Using Array Index as the Key
When rendering lists, React asks for a unique key.
Many beginners simply use the array index because it’s convenient.
It works…
Until the list changes.
If you insert, remove, or reorder items, React can mix them up, causing unexpected UI behavior and unnecessary re-renders.
Better approach
Use a unique and stable identifier from your data, such as a database ID or UUID.
A good key should remain the same even if the list order changes.

3. Overusing useEffect
A surprising number of beginners treat useEffect like a magic solution.
Need to calculate a value?
useEffect.
Need to update something?
useEffect.
Need to synchronize state?
useEffect.
Before long, the component becomes difficult to understand.
The truth is that many things don’t need an effect at all.
If a value can be calculated during rendering, calculate it directly.
Reserve useEffect for actual side effects like:

• Fetching data

• Setting up subscriptions

• Working with timers

• Interacting with browser APIs
Keeping effects minimal makes your components easier to reason about.

4. Making Components Too Large
It’s tempting to build an entire page inside one component.
We’ve all done it.
A few weeks later, that component grows into hundreds of lines containing state, API calls, forms, buttons, modals, and business logic.
Finding a single bug becomes exhausting.
Better approach
Break your UI into smaller, reusable components.
Instead of one massive component, create focused components with a single responsibility.
Smaller components are easier to:

• Read

• Test

• Reuse

• Maintain
If a component feels overwhelming, it’s probably time to split it up.

5. Ignoring Component Reusability
Many beginners copy and paste similar code throughout their application.
It works at first.
But when requirements change, you suddenly have to update the same logic in multiple places.
That’s a maintenance nightmare.
Better approach
Whenever you notice repeated UI or repeated logic, ask yourself:
Can this become a reusable component or custom hook?
React is built around reusability.
The more reusable your code becomes, the faster you’ll build future features.

Bonus Tip: Don’t Rush to Learn Every React Library
One mistake that isn’t talked about enough is trying to learn everything at once.
React Router.
Redux.
Zustand.
TanStack Query.
Next.js.
Tailwind CSS.
Framer Motion.
TypeScript.
The list never ends.
You don’t need to master them all on day one.
Start with the fundamentals:

• Components

• Props

• State

• Events

• Conditional rendering

• Lists

• Hooks
Once you’re comfortable with those, adding new libraries becomes much easier.
A strong foundation will always outperform memorizing dozens of tools.

Final Thoughts
Every experienced React developer has made these mistakes.
The difference isn’t that senior developers never make errors.
It’s that they’ve learned to recognize the patterns early and avoid them.
React isn’t about writing clever code.
It’s about building interfaces that are predictable, maintainable, and easy to understand.
So don’t worry if you’ve made one-or all-of the mistakes in this article.
That’s part of the learning process.
The important thing is to keep building, keep experimenting, and keep improving.
Which React mistake slowed you down the most when you were learning?
I’d love to hear your experience in the comments.
